source.title: Conservation of spatial memory function in the pallial forebrain of reptiles and ray-finned fishes

confidence_rationale: Establishes that the lateral pallium of goldfish (a ray-finned teleost, same class as zebrafish) is functionally homologous to the mammalian hippocampus for spatial memory. LP lesions produce a selective place-task deficit without impairing cue learning -- the hallmark dissociation for hippocampal function. Dorsal and medial pallium lesions do not produce this deficit, confirming anatomical specificity. This is foundational evidence that ARC-007 (hippocampal path memory) is relevant at the zebrafish level and is not a mammalian-only architecture.
failure_signatures: The study uses goldfish, not zebrafish -- the pallial anatomy is broadly conserved across teleosts but not identical.
The lesion approach cannot distinguish between storage and retrieval deficits, or between map-based navigation and other flexible strategies.

The hippocampal homology claim for ray-finned fish pallium is based on functional analogy and developmental marker evidence, not confirmed electrophysiological recordings (no place cells demonstrated in goldfish).

mapping.source_claim_statement: Lateral pallium (LP) lesions in goldfish selectively impair place-task performance (map-based navigation) without affecting cue-based navigation, paralleling hippocampal lesion effects in mammals and birds. Reptile medial cortex lesions show the same dissociation. Neither dorsal nor medial pallium lesions in goldfish produced place-task deficits, confirming LP specificity.
mapping.ree_translation: ARC-007 posits that hippocampal systems store and replay paths through residue-field terrain -- a claim that requires a hippocampal-equivalent structure to be present and functional at the minimal mind level. The Rodriguez data establishes that ray-finned teleost fish (the class including zebrafish) already possess a pallial region functionally homologous to the hippocampus for spatial map-based memory. This means the HippocampalModule in REE has a genuine biological precedent at zebrafish complexity, not just at mammalian complexity. The place-vs-cue dissociation directly mirrors the REE distinction between viability map-based navigation (E3/Hippocampal terrain) and habit-based action selection.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Goldfish to zebrafish is a reasonable transfer within ray-finned teleosts but not identical. More importantly, the paper demonstrates spatial memory storage in the pallium but does not demonstrate replay (SWR-equivalent activity) in that structure during sleep or rest -- the replay component of ARC-007 is not directly evidenced here. This paper supports the substrate claim (hippocampal homolog present) but not the dynamic mechanism claim (replay of paths).
mapping.source_context: Adult goldfish; place task (4-arm maze, spatial cues, new start positions); cue task (visible platform); medial cortex turtle comparison; LP, DP, MP lesion groups; probe and transfer tests.

confidence_components.notes: High source quality (J Neurosci, clean lesion dissociation). Mapping fidelity good for the substrate presence claim; weaker for the dynamic replay component of ARC-007. Transfer risk moderate -- goldfish to zebrafish within teleosts is acceptable; teleost pallium to REE HippocampalModule requires further translation.
